title: Crumpets
subtitle: Griddle-cooked fermented batter rounds full of holes
excerpt: A loose, fermented batter poured into metal rings on a hot griddle. The bubbles that rise as the batter sets create the characteristic porous surface that holds butter. Crumpets are cooked on one side only until the surface is no longer wet, then finished briefly under the grill.
sourceNotes: Crumpets as a griddle-cooked fermented batter round appear in English cookery literature by the 19th century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, records 'crumpets' made with flour, milk, eggs, and yeast, cooked on a griddle in metal rings. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, gives a similar recipe. The distinctive holes are caused by the carbon dioxide released from the yeast during the cook; a small amount of bicarbonate of soda added to the batter before cooking helps open the surface further.

content.text: are produced by yeast gas escaping upward through the batter as it sets. A loose, well-fermented batter and a moderate heat are both needed: too hot and the surface sets before the gas escapes; too loose and the batter runs under the rings. You will need crumpet rings or 9 cm metal round cutters greased on the inside.

attrs.prepNote: dissolved in 1 tbsp warm water, add just before cooking

content.text: Whisk flour, yeast, sugar, and salt together. Add the warm milk and warm water and whisk to a smooth, pourable batter (the consistency of double cream). Cover and leave to ferment at room temperature for 60 minutes. The batter will become bubbly and slightly risen; it will smell lightly of yeast.

Just before cooking, stir in the bicarbonate of soda dissolved in water. Heat a heavy pan over medium heat. Grease the inside of four crumpet rings (or 9 cm cutters) and the surface of the pan with a little butter. Set the rings in the pan and pour about 60 ml of batter into each ring (the batter should fill the ring to about 1 cm depth). Cook on medium heat for 7 to 9 minutes without moving. Holes will form across the surface as the batter sets; the surface is ready when it looks dry and the holes are no longer wet.

Remove the rings and flip the crumpets carefully. Cook the second side for just 2 minutes to set the colour. Transfer to a cooling rack and repeat with the remaining batter. To serve, toast under a grill or in a toaster until the holey surface is golden. Eat with butter.
